# Viewing Logs and Errors

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

![logo cloud IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-cloud-ide.png "Cloud IDE") Cloud IDE

![logo desktop IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-desktop-ide.png "Desktop IDE") Desktop IDE

**Open Beta Release**: The **cloud IDE is in open beta**. Any use of Anypoint Code Builder in its
beta state is subject to the applicable beta services terms and conditions, available from the IDE.

Anypoint Code Builder provides two ways to view logs and errors:

- **ACB Logs** tab: A centralized view that consolidates warnings and errors from all Anypoint Code
  Builder extensions into a single, filterable panel. Use this view to quickly identify and
  troubleshoot issues.
- **Output** panel: Displays detailed, raw logs from individual extension channels. Use this view
  for in-depth investigation when you need complete log output.

## View Errors and Warnings

The **ACB Logs** panel provides a centralized view of all warnings and errors generated by Anypoint
Code Builder. This panel consolidates logs from multiple extension channels into a single,
searchable interface.

To open this panel, click the **ACB Logs** tab in the Panel area, alongside other tabs such as
Output, Problems, and Debug Console.

The panel displays log entries in a table with the following columns:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Column</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Date</strong></p></td><td><p>Timestamp of when the log entry was recorded.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Log Level</strong></p></td><td><p>Severity of the log entry (Error, Warning, Info).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Extension</strong></p></td><td><p>The Anypoint Code Builder extension that generated the log.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Message</strong></p></td><td><p>The detailed text of the log entry.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Project</strong></p></td><td><p>The name of the project related to the log entry.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Filter and Search Logs

Use filters to narrow down the log entries displayed:

- **Level**: Filter by log severity (Error, Warning, Info).
- **Source**: Filter by the extension that generated the log.
- **Date**: Filter by date range.

To search for specific text within the logs, use the search bar.

### Expand Log Details

Click a log entry to expand it and view additional details, such as the full stack trace.

### Clear Logs

To remove all log entries from the panel, click **Clear logs**.

### Export Logs for Troubleshooting

When you export troubleshooting information using the `MuleSoft: Export Troubleshooting Information`
command, Anypoint Code Builder automatically includes the logs in the exported file. For more
information, see [Export Troubleshooting Information](troubleshooting#export-troubleshooting-info).

## View Detailed Logs in the Output Panel

For in-depth investigation, use the Output panel to view raw, detailed logs from individual Anypoint
Code Builder extension channels.

To open and configure the Output panel:

1.  Open the Output panel using one of these methods:
    - Use the keyboard shortcuts: Cmd+Shift+u (Mac) or Ctrl+Shift+u (Windows)
    - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Output**.
    - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
      **Output**:

      ![The View and Output options are displayed in a list of menu choices](_images/select-specific-output-view.png)

2.  In the Output panel, select an output channel from the dropdown list:

    ![A menu bar with the Mule DX Server tab open](_images/select-mule-dx-server.png)

    Common channels include:
    - **Mule DX Server**: General logs from the Anypoint Code Builder backend.
    - **Mule DX Server: Scaffolding**: Logs specific to project scaffolding operations.

The Output panel displays the complete log stream for the selected channel, which is useful for
debugging complex issues that require full context.
---

# Controlling Source Files

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

![logo cloud IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-cloud-ide.png "Cloud IDE") Cloud IDE

![logo desktop IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-desktop-ide.png "Desktop IDE") Desktop IDE

**Open Beta Release**: The **cloud IDE is in open beta**. Any use of Anypoint Code Builder in its
beta state is subject to the applicable beta services terms and conditions, available from the IDE.

Use source control management (SCM) to store and share your design, integration, and implementation
projects. A useful SCM provides change history of each file and a branching methodology to maintain
project quality, full traceability, the ability to restore earlier versions of your files, and a way
to recover source code if your Anypoint Code Builder environment is lost or removed.

GitHub and the Git command line interface (CLI) are widely used in the industry and supported by VS
Code out-of-the-box, with minimal configuration, in the desktop and cloud IDE. The procedures on
this page show how to set up and use GitHub for source control. VS Code extensions for other SCM
systems are also available for you to set up and use. For information, see
[SCM Providers](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/sourcecontrol/overview#_scm-providers).

For more information, see
[Using Git source control in VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/sourcecontrol/overview) in
the VS Code documentation. For detailed information about Git, see the
[Git documentation](https://git-scm.com/book).

## Before You Begin

Ensure that these prerequisites are in place:

- A GitHub account

  If you do not have an account, create one from the [Join GitHub](https://github.com/signup) page
  on the GitHub site.

- A Git installation on your computer

  If Git is not installed on your computer, follow the
  [Git installation instructions](https://github.com/git-guides/install-git) for your machine.

- Access to Anypoint Code Builder from the desktop or cloud IDE

  For setup and installation information, see
  [Getting Started with Anypoint Code Builder](start-acb), and be sure to meet all prerequisites.

- A GitHub user name and email configuration (see
  [Configure Your Git Username and Password](#config-git))

### Configure Your Git Username and Password

Check for and configure your username and email properties so that you can add, update, and retrieve
your project files from a remote Git repo in GitHub.

1.  Meet the prerequisites in [Before You Begin](#prereqs).
2.  From your IDE, open the Terminal window in the console:
    - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Terminal**.
    - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **Terminal** >
      **New Terminal**.
    - For either IDE: Press Ctrl and then press the backtick key (\`).

3.  From the Anypoint Code Builder terminal, check for any existing Git configuration by running
    this command:

    ```command
    git config --list
    ```

    - If your GitHub `user.name` and `user.email` are not listed or if no data returns, configure
      the properties:
      1.  Run this command to set your `user.name`, replacing `YourUserName` with your GitHub
          username:

          ```command
          git config â-global user.name "YourUserName"
          ```

      2.  Run this command to set your `user.email`, replacing `your@email.com` with your GitHub
          email address:

          ```command
          git config â-global user.email "your@email.com"
          ```

          If two-factor authentication is enabled on your GitHub account and you are prompted for a
          password, enter your personal access token instead of your GitHub login password. For more
          information, see GitHub documentation
          [Access GitHub with 2FA](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/securing-your-account-with-two-factor-authentication-2fa/accessing-github-using-two-factor-authentication).

    - If your GitHub `user.name` and `user.email` are listed, you are ready to use Git to control
      your project files from the IDE. For guidance with common source control tasks in VS Code,
      see:
      - [Set Up a Repository from the IDE](#set-up-repo)
      - [Sync Project Changes with Your Repository](#sync-changes)

## Set Up a Repository from the IDE

Set up a repo for your design, integration, or implementation project from the IDE. If your project
is already set up to sync from the IDE with a repo in an SCM that you want to use, no additional
setup is required. You can sync your changes without further setup. For an example, see
[Sync Project Changes with Your Repository](#sync-changes).

To set up a remote GitHub repo from the IDE for your projects, the following scenarios are common:

- [Generate and Publish to a Repository in GitHub](#create-repo)

  If you have a project in Anypoint Code Builder but do not have a repo for that project, you can
  use the IDE to generate a repo in GitHub and publish your project files to that remote repo. The
  process also implicitly initializes a local repo in the IDE.

- [Initialize and Commit Project Files to a Local Repository](#initialize-repo)

  Initializing and committing your project files into a local repo in the IDE is a prerequisite to
  publishing and syncing files into a remote repo in GitHub.

- [Publish Project Files into an Empty Repository in GitHub](#publish-to-empty)

  If you have an empty repo in GitHub, you can publish your project files to the repo from the IDE.

- [Publish Project Files to a Different Repository](#add-extra-repo)

  If your project is already configured to sync with a remote repo, you can configure the IDE to
  publish your project files to a different repo. This feature is helpful, for example, if you want
  to use your own repo for an API spec project that you imported from MuleSoft VCS or if you created
  your design project from the IDE before the February 2024 release. Both types of projects
  automatically add a connection to a remote Git repo in MuleSoft VCS.

- [Clone a Project Repository into the IDE](#clone-repo)

  If a design, implementation, or integration project is already stored in a repo on GitHub, you can
  clone it into the cloud or desktop IDE.

### Generate and Publish to a Repository in GitHub

If you have a project in Anypoint Code Builder but do not have a repo for that project, you can use
the IDE to generate a repo in GitHub and publish your project files to that remote repo. The process
also implicitly initializes a local repo in the IDE. If you already have an empty repo in GitHub
that you intend to use for a project in Anypoint Code Builder, see
[Publish Project Files into an Empty Repository in GitHub](#publish-to-empty) to add and publish to
the repo from the IDE.

1.  Meet the prerequisites in [Before You Begin](#prereqs).
2.  If your Mule project folder is closed, open the project at its _root_ directory:
    1.  Open the Command Palette.

        Show me how
        - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
          - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
          - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

        - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
        - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
          **Command Palette**.

    2.  Provide this command:

        ```command
        File: Open Folder...
        ```

    3.  Navigate to and select the _root_ folder for your project, for example:
        - Integration or Implementation Example
        - Design Project Example

        Example of a root folder and subdirectories of an integration or implementation project in
        Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for an integration or implementation project](_images/int-open-root.png)

        VS Code treats the root as a workspace, which enables the IDE to load the canvas properly.

        Example of the root directory for an API design project in Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for a design project](_images/int-open-root-design.png)

        The root of a project is the top-level folder that Anypoint Code Builder generates when you
        create a project. Do not navigate to the project rootâs parent or ancestor directory or to
        one of its child or descendant directories.

3.  Open the Source Control panel.

    Show me how

    Click the ![](_images/icon-source-control-no-edits.png) (**Source Control**) icon in the
    activity bar or use the keyboard shortcuts:
    - Mac: Ctrl+Shift+g
    - Windows: Cmd+Shift+g

4.  In the Source Control panel, click **Publish to GitHub**.

    ![Publish repo to GitHub from the IDE](_images/start-scm-repo.png)

    When no repo is associated with your project, the IDE provides options for initializing a
    _local_ repo (**Initialize Repository**) on your IDE or directly publishing your project to a
    new remote repo in GitHub (**Publish to GitHub**). Publishing to GitHub also implicitly
    initializes your local repo for the project.

5.  Provide a name for your repo in the field that opens, or use the default name that appears.

    ![Name of new repo](_images/start-scm-repo-name.png)

    The default repo name matches the project name.

6.  Select a repo type (private or public).

    To protect data in your repo, select a private repo (**Publish to GitHub private repo**). This
    process commits and pushes your project files to your new repo.

7.  If prompted, select the files to publish to the repo.

    You can select all or a subset of the files of a design project, for example:

    ![Selecting files to add to the new repo](_images/start-scm-select-files.png)

8.  To view your project files in a GitHub repo, click **Open on GitHub** in the popup.

    ![Opening repo in GitHub](_images/start-scm-repo-open-in-github.png)

    Alternatively, open GitHub from your browser and locate your new repo.

To sync changes to project files with your repo, see
[Sync Project Changes with Your Repository](#sync-changes).

### Initialize and Commit Project Files to a Local Repository

Initializing and committing your project files into a local repo in the IDE is a prerequisite to
publishing and syncing files into a remote repo in GitHub.

1.  Meet the prerequisites in [Before You Begin](#prereqs).
2.  If your Mule project folder is closed, open the project at its _root_ directory:
    1.  Open the Command Palette.

        Show me how
        - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
          - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
          - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

        - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
        - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
          **Command Palette**.

    2.  Provide this command:

        ```command
        File: Open Folder...
        ```

    3.  Navigate to and select the _root_ folder for your project, for example:
        - Integration or Implementation Example
        - Design Project Example

        Example of a root folder and subdirectories of an integration or implementation project in
        Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for an integration or implementation project](_images/int-open-root.png)

        VS Code treats the root as a workspace, which enables the IDE to load the canvas properly.

        Example of the root directory for an API design project in Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for a design project](_images/int-open-root-design.png)

        The root of a project is the top-level folder that Anypoint Code Builder generates when you
        create a project. Do not navigate to the project rootâs parent or ancestor directory or to
        one of its child or descendant directories.

3.  Open the Source Control panel.

    Show me how

    Click the ![](_images/icon-source-control-no-edits.png) (**Source Control**) icon in the
    activity bar or use the keyboard shortcuts:
    - Mac: Ctrl+Shift+g
    - Windows: Cmd+Shift+g

4.  From the Source Control panel for your project, initialize and commit your project files to a
    local repo in the IDE for your project:
    1.  In the Source Control panel, click **Initialize Repository** to generate a local repo for
        your project.

        ![Initializing a local repo for a project](_images/start-scm-repo-initialize.png)

        When no repo is associated with your project, the IDE provides options for initializing a
        _local_ repo (**Initialize Repository**) for your project or directly publishing your
        project to a new remote repo in GitHub (**Publish to GitHub**).

    2.  Stage your files to your local repository by hovering over the **Changes** area (located
        after the **Commit** button) and clicking the ![](_images/icon-scm-add-changes.png) (plus)
        icon.

        ![Adding commit message for staged changes](_images/start-scm-repo-stage-changes.png)

        Alternatively, you can stage a single file by clicking the plus icon for that file.

        Staged files move from the **Changes** area in the Source Control panel to the **Staged
        Changes** area. The UI uses the `git add` command to add changes from your working directory
        to the staging area in your local Git repo.

    3.  Provide your a commit message for the files, and then click **Commit**, for example:

        ![Commit message for staged changes](_images/start-scm-repo-commit-staged.png)

5.  To publish your files to a remote repository, you can:
    - [Publish Project Files into an Empty Repository in GitHub](#publish-to-empty)

      If you have an empty repo in GitHub, you can publish your project files to the repo from the
      IDE.

    - [Publish Project Files to a Different Repository](#add-extra-repo)

      If your project is already configured to sync with a remote repo, you can configure the IDE to
      publish your project files to a different repo.

### Publish Project Files into an Empty Repository in GitHub

If you have an empty repo in GitHub, you can publish your project files to the repo from the IDE.
This procedure assumes that the project files are ready to stage and publish to the repo.

1.  Meet the prerequisites in [Before You Begin](#prereqs).
2.  If you have not yet initialized a local repo for your project in the IDE, see
    [Initialize and Commit Project Files to a Local Repository](#initialize-repo).
3.  Open the Command Palette.

    Show me how
    - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
      - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
      - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

    - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
    - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
      **Command Palette**.

4.  Configure a connection from the project in the IDE to your empty remote repo in GitHub:
    1.  Provide the following command to add the remote repo on GitHub:

        ```command
        Git: Add Remote...
        ```

    2.  Select **Add Remote from GitHub** from the popup menu.

        ![Adding an remote repo from GitHub](_images/start-scm-oas-add-remote.png)

    3.  Provide the URL to your git repo, for example:

        ![Adding a remote repo URL from GitHub](_images/start-scm-repo-add-remote-uri.png)

    4.  Provide a name for the remote in the IDE, for example:

        my-acb-project-repo

        The name for the repo in the IDE can match or differ from the repo name in GitHub.

5.  Click **Publish Branch**.

    ![Publish branch files in a repo on GitHub](_images/start-scm-repo-publish-branch.png)

6.  Open your repo in GitHub to review the published files, for example:

    ![Project files in a repo on GitHub](_images/start-scm-repo-github.png)

To sync changes to project files with your repo, see
[Sync Project Changes with Your Repository](#sync-changes).

### Publish Project Files to a Different Repository

If your project is already configured to sync with a remote repo, you can configure the IDE to
publish your project files to a different repo. This feature is helpful, for example, if you want to
use your own repo for an API spec project that you imported from MuleSoft VCS or if you created your
design project from the IDE before the February 2024 release. Both types of projects automatically
add a connection to a remote Git repo in MuleSoft VCS. For more information, see
[Source Control for API Design Projects](des-designing-api-specs#anypoint-scm).

If you are unsure whether your project is already connected to a remote repo, see
[Check for a Remote Repository URL](#check-remote).

This example assumes that you have an empty repo in GitHub. If you do not, you can create one from
GitHub. For guidance, see
[Quickstart for repositories](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/creating-and-managing-repositories/quickstart-for-repositories)
in the GitHub documentation.

1.  Meet the prerequisites in [Before You Begin](#prereqs).
2.  If your Mule project folder is closed, open the project at its _root_ directory:
    1.  Open the Command Palette.

        Show me how
        - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
          - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
          - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

        - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
        - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
          **Command Palette**.

    2.  Provide this command:

        ```command
        File: Open Folder...
        ```

    3.  Navigate to and select the _root_ folder for your project, for example:
        - Integration or Implementation Example
        - Design Project Example

        Example of a root folder and subdirectories of an integration or implementation project in
        Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for an integration or implementation project](_images/int-open-root.png)

        VS Code treats the root as a workspace, which enables the IDE to load the canvas properly.

        Example of the root directory for an API design project in Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for a design project](_images/int-open-root-design.png)

        The root of a project is the top-level folder that Anypoint Code Builder generates when you
        create a project. Do not navigate to the project rootâs parent or ancestor directory or to
        one of its child or descendant directories.

3.  Open the Source Control panel.

    Show me how

    Click the ![](_images/icon-source-control.png) (**Source Control**) icon in the activity bar or
    use the keyboard shortcuts:
    - Mac: Ctrl+Shift+g
    - Windows: Cmd+Shift+g
    1.  In the Source Control Panel, notice that there is a repo in the **Source Control
        Repositories** area of the panel, for example:

        ![Project source control using MuleSoft VCS](_images/start-scm-oas-anypoint.png)

4.  Open the Command Palette.

    Show me how
    - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
      - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
      - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

    - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
    - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
      **Command Palette**.

5.  Configure a connection from the project in the IDE to your repo in GitHub:
    1.  Provide the following command to add the remote repo on GitHub:

        ```command
        Git: Add Remote...
        ```

    2.  Select **Add Remote from GitHub** from the popup menu.

        ![Adding an extra remote repo from GitHub](_images/start-scm-oas-add-remote.png)

    3.  Provide the URL to your git repo, for example:

        https://github.com/me/my-extra-repo.git

    4.  Provide a name for the remote in the IDE, for example:

        my-extra-repo

        This name for the repo in the IDE can match or differ from the repo name in GitHub.

6.  Publish your project to your extra remote repo:
    1.  In the command palette, provide the following command:

        ```command
        Git: Push to...
        ```

    2.  Select **Git: Push toâ¦â** from the popup menu.

        ![Selecting 'Push toâ¦â from the IDE'](_images/start-scm-oas-push-to.png)

    3.  Select the extra remote repo that you added, for example:

        ![Selecting the extra remote repo](_images/start-scm-oas-publish-to-private-repo.png)

7.  Check for your project source files in your repo on GitHub, for example:

    ![Viewing project files in the extra remote repo](_images/start-scm-oas-in-github.png)

You can now clone this project repo in your IDE to easily modify and sync your changes from the
IDEâs UI. See [Clone a Project Repository into the IDE](#clone-repo).

## Sync Project Changes with Your Repository

After your project is connected and published to a remote repo in GitHub, you can use the IDE to
sync your changes with that repo. If your repo is not set up yet, see
[Set Up a Repository from the IDE](#set-up-repo).

Use the IDEâs UI or its built-in terminal to sync your changes:

- [Sync Changes from the Source Control Panel](#sync-from-ui)
- [Sync Changes from the Command Terminal](#sync-from-terminal)

### Sync Changes from the Source Control Panel

To sync changes to your files from your projectâs Source Control panel in the IDE:

1.  If your Mule project folder is closed, open the project at its _root_ directory:
    1.  Open the Command Palette.

        Show me how
        - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
          - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
          - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

        - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
        - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
          **Command Palette**.

    2.  Provide this command:

        ```command
        File: Open Folder...
        ```

    3.  Navigate to and select the _root_ folder for your project, for example:
        - Integration or Implementation Example
        - Design Project Example

        Example of a root folder and subdirectories of an integration or implementation project in
        Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for an integration or implementation project](_images/int-open-root.png)

        VS Code treats the root as a workspace, which enables the IDE to load the canvas properly.

        Example of the root directory for an API design project in Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for a design project](_images/int-open-root-design.png)

        The root of a project is the top-level folder that Anypoint Code Builder generates when you
        create a project. Do not navigate to the project rootâs parent or ancestor directory or to
        one of its child or descendant directories.

2.  Open the Source Control panel.

    Show me how

    Click the ![](_images/icon-source-control.png) (**Source Control**) icon in the activity bar or
    use the keyboard shortcuts:
    - Mac: Ctrl+Shift+g
    - Windows: Cmd+Shift+g

3.  In the Source Control panel:
    1.  Stage your files to your local repository by hovering over the **Changes** area (located
        after the **Commit** button) and clicking the ![](_images/icon-scm-add-changes.png) (plus)
        icon.

        ![Adding commit message for staged changes](_images/start-scm-repo-stage-changes.png)

        Alternatively, you can stage a single file by clicking the plus icon for that file.

        Staged files move from the **Changes** area in the Source Control panel to the **Staged
        Changes** area. The UI uses the `git add` command to add changes from your working directory
        to the staging area in your local Git repo.

    2.  Provide your a commit message for the files, and then click **Commit**, for example:

        ![Commit message for staged changes](_images/start-scm-repo-commit-staged.png)

    3.  Click **Sync Changes** to push your changes to your remote repo on GitHub.

4.  Open the project in GitHub to check for the synced changes.

### Sync Changes from the Command Terminal

To sync changes to your files from your projectâs command terminal in the IDE:

1.  If your Mule project folder is closed, open the project at its _root_ directory:
    1.  Open the Command Palette.

        Show me how
        - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
          - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
          - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

        - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
        - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
          **Command Palette**.

    2.  Provide this command:

        ```command
        File: Open Folder...
        ```

    3.  Navigate to and select the _root_ folder for your project, for example:
        - Integration or Implementation Example
        - Design Project Example

        Example of a root folder and subdirectories of an integration or implementation project in
        Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for an integration or implementation project](_images/int-open-root.png)

        VS Code treats the root as a workspace, which enables the IDE to load the canvas properly.

        Example of the root directory for an API design project in Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for a design project](_images/int-open-root-design.png)

        The root of a project is the top-level folder that Anypoint Code Builder generates when you
        create a project. Do not navigate to the project rootâs parent or ancestor directory or to
        one of its child or descendant directories.

2.  From your IDE, open the Terminal window in the console:
    - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Terminal**.
    - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **Terminal** >
      **New Terminal**.
    - For either IDE: Press Ctrl and then press the backtick key (\`).

3.  At the prompt, run Git commands to add new files to your repo and to stage, commit, and push
    changes to your remote repo on GitHub.

    The following example uses `git status` to check the status of changes, `git commit` to commit
    all changed files and add a commit message, and `git push` to merge the changes into the files
    on the remote repo.

    $ git status On branch main Your branch is up to date with '011124-test-api-remote/main'.

    Changes not staged for commit: (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed) (use
    "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory) modified: 011124-test-api.raml

    no changes added to commit (use "git add" and/or "git commit -a")

    $ git commit -am 'my commit message' \[main bccc296\] another commit 1 file changed, 1
    deletion(-)

    $ git push Enumerating objects: 5, done. Counting objects: 100% (5/5), done. Delta compression
    using up to 8 threads Compressing objects: 100% (3/3), done. Writing objects: 100% (3/3), 944
    bytes | 944.00 KiB/s, done. Total 3 (delta 2), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0 remote:
    Resolving deltas: 100% (2/2), completed with 2 local objects. To
    https://github.com/me/my-repo.git e4156cc..bccc296 main -> main

For common Git commands, see [About Git](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/using-git/about-git)
in the GitHub documentation, or find all commands in the
[Git command reference](https://git-scm.com/docs).

## Clone a Project Repository into the IDE

If your design, implementation, or integration project is already stored in a repo on GitHub, you
and your team members can clone it into Anypoint Code Builder desktop or cloud IDEs. Cloning is
helpful for sharing your project with another IDE, for enabling you to edit source and sync changes
from the IDE, and for retrieving a backup of a deleted project. For example, you can work on the
same project in the desktop and cloud IDE if your project is stored in a repo to which you and your
team have access.

1.  From your IDE, open the Terminal window in the console:
    - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Terminal**.
    - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **Terminal** >
      **New Terminal**.
    - For either IDE: Press Ctrl and then press the backtick key (\`).

2.  Open the Command Palette.

    Show me how
    - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
      - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
      - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

    - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
    - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
      **Command Palette**.

3.  Clone the repo into the Source Control panel for your project:
    1.  Provide the following command to add the remote repo on GitHub:

        ```command
        Git: Clone
        ```

    2.  Provide the URL to your repo in GitHub, for example:

        ```command
        https://github.com/_owner_/_yourrepo_.git
        ```

        Replace the URL in the example with the URL to your repo in GitHub. The owner and repo URL
        are unique to your GitHub site.

    3.  Navigate to a directory for your new repo, and click **Select as repo Destination**.
    4.  If prompted with the request **Would you like to open the cloned repository?**, click
        **Open** to open the project.

        For cloned integration or implementation projects, you can navigate to `src`â `main` â
        `mule` to find the config XML file or files.

To sync changes to project files with your repo, see
[Sync Project Changes with Your Repository](#sync-changes).

## Check for a Remote Repository URL

To determine whether a remote GitHub repo for your project is configured in the IDE, check for a URL
to the repo:

1.  If your Mule project folder is closed, open the project at its _root_ directory:
    1.  Open the Command Palette.

        Show me how
        - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
          - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
          - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

        - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
        - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
          **Command Palette**.

    2.  Provide this command:

        ```command
        File: Open Folder...
        ```

    3.  Navigate to and select the _root_ folder for your project, for example:
        - Integration or Implementation Example
        - Design Project Example

        Example of a root folder and subdirectories of an integration or implementation project in
        Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for an integration or implementation project](_images/int-open-root.png)

        VS Code treats the root as a workspace, which enables the IDE to load the canvas properly.

        Example of the root directory for an API design project in Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for a design project](_images/int-open-root-design.png)

        The root of a project is the top-level folder that Anypoint Code Builder generates when you
        create a project. Do not navigate to the project rootâs parent or ancestor directory or to
        one of its child or descendant directories.

2.  From your IDE, open the Terminal window in the console:
    - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Terminal**.
    - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **Terminal** >
      **New Terminal**.
    - For either IDE: Press Ctrl and then press the backtick key (\`).

3.  Provide the following command:

    ```command
    git remote -v
    ```

    - If the project has one or more remote repo configurations, the command returns the URL for
      each remote, along with `push` and `fetch` information, for example:

      $ git remote -v origin https://github.com/me/my-project-repo.git (fetch) origin
      https://github.com/me/my-project-repo.git (push)

    - If no results return, a remote repo is not configured for the project.
    - If the error `fatal: not a git repo (or any of the parent directories): .git` returns, a local
      repo is not initialized for the project.

For information about `git remote` command options, see
[git-remote reference](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-remote) in the GitHub documentation.

## Remove a Remote Repository from Your Project

To remove a remote repo from your project workspace:

1.  If your Mule project folder is closed, open the project at its _root_ directory:
    1.  Open the Command Palette.

        Show me how
        - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
          - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
          - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

        - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
        - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
          **Command Palette**.

    2.  Provide this command:

        ```command
        File: Open Folder...
        ```

    3.  Navigate to and select the _root_ folder for your project, for example:
        - Integration or Implementation Example
        - Design Project Example

        Example of a root folder and subdirectories of an integration or implementation project in
        Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for an integration or implementation project](_images/int-open-root.png)

        VS Code treats the root as a workspace, which enables the IDE to load the canvas properly.

        Example of the root directory for an API design project in Anypoint Code Builder:

        ![Project folder for a design project](_images/int-open-root-design.png)

        The root of a project is the top-level folder that Anypoint Code Builder generates when you
        create a project. Do not navigate to the project rootâs parent or ancestor directory or to
        one of its child or descendant directories.

2.  Open the Source Control panel.

    Show me how

    Click the ![](_images/icon-source-control.png) (**Source Control**) icon in the activity bar or
    use the keyboard shortcuts:
    - Mac: Ctrl+Shift+g
    - Windows: Cmd+Shift+g

3.  In the Source Control panel:
    1.  Provide the following Git command to add the remote repo on GitHub:

        ```command
        Git: Remove Remote...
        ```

    2.  In the popup, select the remote repo that you want to remove.

        In the IDE, your local Git repo remains because it was initialized before syncing with the
        remote.

## See Also

- [Introduction to Git in VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/sourcecontrol/quickstart)

# Using the Command Palette

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

![logo cloud IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-cloud-ide.png "Cloud IDE") Cloud IDE

![logo desktop IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-desktop-ide.png "Desktop IDE") Desktop IDE

**Open Beta Release**: The **cloud IDE is in open beta**. Any use of Anypoint Code Builder in its
beta state is subject to the applicable beta services terms and conditions, available from the IDE.

The Command Palette in Anypoint Code Builder is the control center for all commands you use to
perform tasks. Knowing how to open it is important for your productivity.

1.  Open the Command Palette in one of the following ways:
    - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
      - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
      - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

    - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
    - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
      **Command Palette**:

      ![The View and Command Palette options are displayed in a list of menu choices](_images/open-command-palette.png)

2.  Type `MuleSoft:` and browse the available commands:

    ![A text box for entering commands, with a list of recently used and other commands](_images/all-mulesoft-commands.png)

For a list of MuleSoft commands, see [Command Reference](ref-acb-commands).

# Working with Workspaces

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

![logo cloud IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-cloud-ide.png "Cloud IDE") Cloud IDE

![logo desktop IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-desktop-ide.png "Desktop IDE") Desktop IDE

**Open Beta Release**: The **cloud IDE is in open beta**. Any use of Anypoint Code Builder in its
beta state is subject to the applicable beta services terms and conditions, available from the IDE.

Organize your Mule projects in workspaces to ensure all Anypoint Code Builder features work
correctly. Workspaces help you manage multiple related projects and avoid build-time and design-time
issues.

## Valid Setups for Anypoint Code Builder

Anypoint Code Builder requires one of the following project configurations to function properly:

- A single Mule project open at the root of your directory
- A multi-root workspace pointing directly to one or more Mule projects

If your directory structure doesnât meet these requirements, Anypoint Code Builder displays a
message with steps to resolve the issue, such as opening a Mule project or creating a workspace. See
[Fix a Nested Project Error](#fix-nested-project).

## Create a Project in a Workspace

When you create a new project, add it to a workspace. With this setup you can add more projects
later without restructuring your environment.

To create a project in a workspace:

1.  Start the project creation process using one of the following methods:
    - From the **Create** menu, select the project type.

      For example, **Implement an API** or **Develop an Integration**.

    - From the Command Palette, run the appropriate command.

      For example, `MuleSoft: Implement an API Specification`.

    - Ask MuleSoft Vibes to create the project using natural language.

2.  In the project creation form, select **Add to workspace**.

    You can create a new workspace or add the project to an existing workspace.

3.  Complete the remaining project configuration options.
4.  Click **Create Project**.

    Anypoint Code Builder creates the project and adds it to the selected workspace.

## Import an Anypoint Studio Workspace

If you have existing projects in Anypoint Studio, you can import your Studio workspace into Anypoint
Code Builder.

To import a Studio workspace:

1.  Open the Command Palette.

    Show me how
    - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
      - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
      - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

    - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
    - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
      **Command Palette**.

2.  Select the following command:

    ```command
    MuleSoft: Import Studio Workspace
    ```

3.  Navigate to the location of your Studio workspace and select it.
4.  Choose a location to save the new workspace file.

    Anypoint Code Builder creates a workspace that references your existing Studio projects. Changes
    made to the original Studio workspace are automatically reflected in Anypoint Code Builder.

## Open and Switch Between Workspaces

You can open or switch between workspaces using the menu, the Command Palette, or MuleSoft Vibes.

### Open a Workspace Manually

1.  Open the workspace file using one of these methods:
    - From the menu bar, select **File** > **Open Workspace from Fileâ¦â**.
    - From the Command Palette, run the command `File: Open Workspace from Fileâ¦â`.

2.  Navigate to the location where you saved the `.code-workspace` file (for example, your home
    directory or a dedicated workspaces folder), and select it.

    The projects open in the Explorer view, and the folder name for the workspace includes
    **(WORKSPACE)** to indicate that youâre working in a multi-root workspace:

    ![A multi-root workspace in Explorer view](_images/imp-local-api-open-workspace.png)

### Open a Workspace with MuleSoft Vibes

You can ask MuleSoft Vibes to switch workspaces using natural language, such as:

- "Open my Orders API workspace"
- "Switch to the `<YourWorkspaceName>` workspace"

## Close a Workspace

To close the current workspace:

1.  Open the Command Palette.

    Show me how
    - Use the keyboard shortcuts:
      - Mac: Cmd+Shift+p
      - Windows: Ctrl+Shift+p

    - In the desktop IDE, select **View** > **Command Palette**.
    - In the cloud IDE, click the ![](_images/icon-menu.png) (menu) icon, and select **View** >
      **Command Palette**.

2.  Select the following command:

    ```command
    Workspaces: Close Workspace
    ```

## Actions in Workspaces

Anypoint Code Builder organizes actions based on their scope:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Action Type</th><th>Location</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Global actions</strong></p></td><td><p>Always available from the Command Palette. These actions donât depend on a specific project, such as creating a new project, importing assets from Exchange, or opening Anypoint Code Builder settings.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Project-specific actions</strong></p></td><td><p>Available from the context menu (right-click) in the Explorer view. These actions apply to a specific project, such as running, debugging, deploying, or exporting a project.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

When working with multiple projects in a workspace, select the target project before running
project-specific actions. For example, when you run or debug, you must select which project in the
workspace to execute.

## Workspace File Location

VS Code stores workspace configuration in a file with the `.code-workspace` extension. This file
maps the folders for your projects.

> [!IMPORTANT] The `.code-workspace` file must not reside within any of your project folders. Save
> it in a separate directory, such as your home directory or a dedicated workspaces folder.

## Fix a Nested Project Error

This error appears when you open a folder that contains your Mule project instead of opening the
project directly, and that folder isnât configured as a multi-root workspace.

To fix this issue, open the project folder at the root level or add it to a multi-root workspace.

### Open the Project at the Root

If youâre working with a single project, open it directly so it appears at the top level of the
Explorer view.

1.  From the menu bar, select **File** > **Open Folderâ¦â**.
2.  Navigate to your Mule projectâs root folder (the folder containing the `pom.xml` file).
3.  Select the folder and click **Open**.

    The project now appears at the top level of the Explorer view, and Anypoint Code Builder
    features are available.

### Add the Project to a Multi-Root Workspace

If you want to keep your current folder structure or work with multiple projects, create a
multi-root workspace that points directly to your Mule project.

1.  From the menu bar, select **File** > **Add Folder to Workspaceâ¦â**.
2.  Navigate to your Mule projectâs root folder and select it.
3.  When prompted, save the workspace file (`.code-workspace`) to a location outside of your project
    folders.

    The project appears in the Explorer view with the **(WORKSPACE)** indicator, and Anypoint Code
    Builder features are available.

> [!TIP] If you frequently work with multiple projects, creating a workspace from the start avoids
> this issue. See [Create a Project in a Workspace](#create-project-workspace).

## See Also

- [Iteratively Design and Implement APIs](imp-implement-local-apis)
- [Creating Integrations](int-create-integrations)
- [Creating and Importing API Specifications](des-create-api-specs)
- [Multi-root Workspaces](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/editor/multi-root-workspaces) in the VS
  Code documentation
---

# Authentication Errors

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

![logo cloud IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-cloud-ide.png "Cloud IDE") Cloud IDE

![logo desktop IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-desktop-ide.png "Desktop IDE") Desktop IDE

**Open Beta Release**: The **cloud IDE is in open beta**. Any use of Anypoint Code Builder in its
beta state is subject to the applicable beta services terms and conditions, available from the IDE.

In Anypoint Code Builder, authentication errors sometimes occur when you attempt to perform actions
that require a connection to Anypoint Platform or to services that the cloud and desktop IDEs rely
on.

## Failed to Authenticate to Git

All commands that involve `git` actions, such as publishing to Exchange and committing changes to an
API specification, require authentication with the correct Anypoint Platform host cloud. If
authentication fails, the action throws this VS Code error:

Failed to authenticate to git remote

To address this issue, cancel the error notification, and ensure that your IDE is set to the correct
region for your project and that you are logged in to Anypoint Platform.
---

# Disk Space Issues in Cloud IDE

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

![logo cloud IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-cloud-ide.png "Cloud IDE") Cloud IDE

**Open Beta Release**: The **cloud IDE is in open beta**. Any use of Anypoint Code Builder in its
beta state is subject to the applicable beta services terms and conditions, available from the IDE.

When the Cloud IDE runs out of disk space, the devcontainer can present issues. Use this recovery
sequence to restore access and prevent repeated startup failures.

Cloud IDE disk space issues can manifest as:

- The IDE remains in an indefinite **Connecting** state.
- Project creation fails, or new projects donât load.
- Terminal or log output shows `ENOENT: no such file or directory`, indicating a file or directory
  is missing.

In such cases, follow the recovery sequence to restore access and prevent repeated startup failures.

## Reboot the Cloud IDE

Reboot the Cloud IDE first to recover from transient startup issues.

1.  Open Anypoint Code Builder Cloud IDE.
2.  Select **Manage your IDE**.
3.  Select **Reboot**.
4.  Wait 5 to 6 minutes for the reboot to complete.
5.  Select **Launch**, and verify that you can access the Cloud IDE.

## Free Up Space

If the reboot doesnât resolve the issue, remove content you no longer need from the workspace.

- Delete unused Mule project folders from the Cloud IDE workspace.
- Remove files and folders that are no longer required for active development.

## Delete and Recreate the Cloud IDE

If the issue persists, as a last resort, recreate the Cloud IDE instance from the Anypoint Code
Builder launch page.

> [!IMPORTANT] Deleting the Cloud IDE instance permanently removes local project files. Back up or
> download important project folders before you continue.

1.  Go to the Anypoint Code Builder launch page.
2.  Delete the Cloud IDE instance.
3.  Wait 1 minute.
4.  Click **Launch** to create a new Cloud IDE instance.
---

# DataWeave Issues and Errors

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

![logo cloud IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-cloud-ide.png "Cloud IDE") Cloud IDE

![logo desktop IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-desktop-ide.png "Desktop IDE") Desktop IDE

**Open Beta Release**: The **cloud IDE is in open beta**. Any use of Anypoint Code Builder in its
beta state is subject to the applicable beta services terms and conditions, available from the IDE.

When Anypoint Code Builder canât process a value, the IDE provides a visual indicator to identify an
issue with the value. Issues include:

- [Unable to find sample input](#sample-input)
- [Unable to resolve reference](#resolve-reference)
- [Expects _x_ but got _y_](#expects-y)
- [Invalid input](#invalid-input)

## Unable to Find Sample Input

The DataWeave error `Unable to find sample input` applies to a Mule event variable, such as
`payload`. In this case, the IDE places a wavy blue line under the value in the configuration XML
file.

!['Unable to find sample input' error](_images/dw-issue-sample-data.png)

If your application isnât running, the IDE canât provide any information about Mule variables unless
you provide sample data. Use [Quick Fix](int-configure-dw-expressions#sample-data) to provide sample
data in the format and data structure that you expect to be present when the application runs.

For more information about Mule event variables, see
[Predefined Variables](../dataweave/latest/dataweave-variables-context).

## Unable to Resolve Reference

The error `Unable to resolve reference` is typically for a sequence of characters like `afkdldjf`
that isnât recognized.

![`Unable to resolve reference` error](_images/dw-issue-reference-resolution.png)

Either provide the intended value or use [Quick Fix](int-configure-dw-expressions#sample-data) to
convert the character sequence into a DataWeave variable. For more information, see
[DataWeave Variables](../dataweave/latest/dataweave-variables).

## Expects _x_ but Got _y_

The error similar to `Expects _x_ but got _y_` typically indicates that something is wrong with the
provided function. For example, `Expects 1 argument but got 0` occurs for `round()` because the
function is missing an argument, in this case, a number to round. An example of a fix is
`round(1.65)`. Similar errors indicate that an argument is of the wrong data type or that a function
is otherwise malformed and requires correction.

Read the error message to determine what part of the expression to fix, and review the function
description after the error message to learn more about the function. See
[DataWeave Modules](../dataweave/latest/dw-functions#dw_modules) to find additional DataWeave
function documentation.

## Invalid Input

The error `Invalid input` typically occurs when special characters, such as `?`, appear in a
DataWeave expression and arenât escaped, for example:

!['Invalid input' error](_images/dw-issue-invalid-input.png)

For more information, see
[Rules for Declaring Valid Identifiers](../dataweave/latest/dataweave-language-introduction#valid-identifiers)
and
[Escape Special Characters](../dataweave/latest/dataweave-language-introduction#escape-special-characters).
---

# Deployment Errors

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

![logo cloud IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-cloud-ide.png "Cloud IDE") Cloud IDE

![logo desktop IDE](../_/img/logos/deployment-desktop-ide.png "Desktop IDE") Desktop IDE

**Open Beta Release**: The **cloud IDE is in open beta**. Any use of Anypoint Code Builder in its
beta state is subject to the applicable beta services terms and conditions, available from the IDE.

In Anypoint Code Builder, the error `Failed to deploy to CloudHub: 400` occurs when you deploy a
Mule application to CloudHub if the root organization or business group to which you are deploying
lacks a sufficient vCore allocation.

To address this isssue, increase the vCore allocation to the root organization or business group in
the **Sandbox** environment:

- To reallocate resources, see
  [Redistribute Resources Between Existing Business Groups](../access-management/business-groups#redistribute-resources).
- To manage environments, see [Anypoint Platform Environments](../access-management/environments).
  For additional help, contact your organization administrator for Anypoint Platform.

# Troubleshoot Generative AI for Anypoint Code Builder

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The following errors sometimes occur when you use Generative AI for Anypoint Code Builder.

## Unexpected Query Results When Using the Generative AI Feature

If the results returned by the agent arenât what you want, make sure your message includes the
required information the agent needs to
[design your API spec](vibes-api-ai-create-spec#design-spec-ai). Consider adding the optional
details provided in the API spec design guidance as well.

## Troubleshoot Access Errors

If you canât access generative AI, contact your Anypoint Platform organization administrator. If
youâre the organization administrator, check these considerations:

- You accepted the terms and conditions and
  [enabled Einstein for Anypoint Code Builder](../access-management/enabling-agentforce) for your
  organization.
- The Salesforce organization youâre connected to for requests has generative AI enabled. For more
  information, see
  [Set Up Einstein Generative AI](https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=sf.generative_ai_enable.htm).
- The Salesforce organization youâre connected to for requests has an
  [established a tenant relationship](../access-management/trusted-salesforce-org) with your
  Anypoint Platform organization, and the connection is enabled.
- The Salesforce organization youâre connected to for requests is valid. For example, verify that
  itâs not expired or otherwise disabled.

## Troubleshoot Anypoint Platform Authorization Errors

If you receive an authorization error, contact your Anypoint Platform organization administrator. If
youâre the organization administrator, check the following:

- The user has the **Mule Developer Generative AI User** permission assigned in Anypoint Platform
  access management.
- You accepted the terms and conditions to
  [enable Einstein](../access-management/enabling-agentforce) for your organization.
- Your Anypoint Platform organization is current and active.

## Generative AI Doesnât Show for EU Cloud Users on the Desktop IDE

If youâre signed in to your Anypoint Platform account on the EU Cloud and generative AI doesnât show
in the UI, verify these are correct:

- You completed the [Set the Desktop IDE to the EU or US Cloud](start-acb#change-clouds) procedure.
- Youâre signed in to Anypoint Platform through the Anypoint Code Builder app.
- [You have the required permissions](vibes-api-ai-create-spec#before-you-begin).

## Troubleshoot Chat Availability

Generative AI requires that a supported file is open in the editor when you open the panel. If this
condition isnât met, you might see one of the following messages:

1.  "To start a new chat for API, open a compatible API specification."  
    This message appears when youâre in an API project but the active file is either not an API
    specification or is written in an unsupported format. To resolve this issue, open an API spec
    file in RAML 1.0 or OAS 3.0 format.
2.  "To start a new chat for Flow, open an integration project."  
    This message appears when youâre in an API project or in an integration project but no .xml file
    is selected. To fix it, open a .xml file that is part of an integration project.

If youâre in an integration project and have an API spec open, the panel shows, but you wonât be
able to generate APIs. In this case, you can only use generative AI to generate Flows. To generate
APIs, switch to an API project and open a supported API specification file.

If the file you have open is in OpenAPI 2.0 or another unsupported format, you canât use generative
AI. Open a file that uses RAML 1.0 or OAS 3.0 instead.

Generative AI determines the output format based on the file that was in focus when the panel was
opened. If a RAML 1.0 file is active, the generated content is in RAML 1.0. If an OAS 3.0 file is
active, the generated content is in OAS 3.0.

## See Also

- [Salesforce Organization Connections in Anypoint Platform](../access-management/trusted-salesforce-org)
- [Enabling or Disabling Agentforce for Anypoint Platform](../access-management/enabling-agentforce)
- [Log in to Anypoint Platform from the IDE](start-acb#log-in-to-anypoint-platform-from-the-ide)
- [Anypoint Code Builder Permissions](../access-management/permissions-by-product#anypoint-code-builder)
